conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. They're all young - Alex is 3, Blake is 2, and Charlie is 3 as well.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex (age 3), Blake (age 2), and Charlie (age 3).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to ask you a few more questions.

First, are you responsible for all three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, it's a bit complicated. Blake lives with me at home. But Alex and Charlie don't live with me - they live with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. It's important to understand the living arrangements for each child.

Since Blake lives with you, let me ask about Alex and Charlie who live with their other parent: Do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay (currently at least £25.60 a week) towards the cost of looking after Alex and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, yes, I do contribute to their upkeep - I help out with things like clothes, toys, and other things they need. I give about £26 a week.

For Charlie, no, I don't contribute that much. Their other parent takes care of most things.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

First, regarding Blake who lives with you: Are you the person primarily responsible for Blake, or does Blake live with someone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake lives with me and I'm the one looking after them. Nobody else is claiming for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand a bit more about Alex and Charlie who live with their other parent.

For Alex, where you do contribute at least £26 a week - is their other parent (or anyone else) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: For Alex, no, the other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for them. But for Charlie, yes, the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that detail. I have a few final questions to complete the assessment.

Are you currently living or working in the UK, or do you have any connection to the UK such as being subject to UK tax?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one more question to complete the assessment:

Are any of the three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently in local authority care, or are any of them in hospital for more than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are in care or in hospital. They're all healthy and not in any of those situations.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

Let me provide you with the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Based on the information you've provided, here's your Child Benefit eligibility for each child:

**✅ Alex - ELIGIBLE**
Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you are contributing at least £26 per week towards their upkeep, which meets the minimum requirement. Since their other parent is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you can claim.

**✅ Blake - ELIGIBLE**
Blake lives with you, and you're the primary carer. Nobody else is claiming for Blake, so you can claim Child Benefit for them.

**❌ Charlie - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Charlie's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and you don't contribute at least the minimum amount towards Charlie's upkeep, you cannot claim for Charlie.

You can claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake. If you'd like to proceed with claiming, you can apply online through the GOV.UK website or by post.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, which you would need to consider.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 3 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex does not live with you but lives with their other parent. However, you are contributing at least £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay (currently £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child). According to UK Child Benefit rules, you qualify as being 'responsible for a child' if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). Additionally, the other parent is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, so there is no conflict. Since Alex is under 16, you live in the UK, and meet the responsibility test through your financial contributions, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake lives with you and you are primarily responsible for them. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Since Blake is 2 years old (under 16), lives with you, you live in the UK, and no one else is claiming for Blake, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie does not live with you but lives with their other parent. You stated that you do not contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay towards Charlie's upkeep. According to UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and you would need to either live with the child or contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep to be considered responsible for them. Since Charlie's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, and only one person can claim for the same child, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie, all under age 4). Blake lives with the claimant and no one else is claiming, making them eligible for Blake. Alex lives with the other parent, but the claimant contributes at least £26/week (meeting the minimum threshold) and the other parent is not claiming, making them eligible for Alex. Charlie lives with the other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit, and the claimant does not contribute the minimum amount, making them ineligible for Charlie as only one person can claim per child.
